text:
CT NECK WITH CONTRAST — SURVEILLANCE

CLINICAL INDICATION:
53-year-old female with history of HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, staged T2N1M0 at diagnosis, treated with definitive concurrent chemoradiation (IMRT 70 Gy / 35 fractions with concurrent cis x2 cycles). Post-treatment PET/CT at 12 weeks demonstrated complete metabolic response. Present study obtained as part of routine oncologic surveillance approximately 18 months following completion of chemoradiation.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the neck was performed from the skull base through the thoracic inlet following administration of intravenous iodinated contrast (Omnipaque 350, 100 mL, power injector). Axial acquisitions were obtained in the portal venous phase with reformatted coronal and sagittal images generated. Slice thickness 1.25 mm. Soft tissue and bone algorithm reconstructions reviewed.

COMPARISON:
CT neck with contrast obtained approximately 6 months prior (12-month post-treatment surveillance study).

FINDINGS:

Oropharynx and Primary Site:
The right base of tongue demonstrates expected post-radiation changes including mild mucosal thickening and subtle submucosal soft tissue stranding, essentially unchanged in character compared with the prior study. There is no discrete enhancing mass or focal nodular soft tissue abnormality to suggest recurrent or residual neoplasm at the primary site. The right glossoepiglottic fold shows mild residual mucosal irregularity in keeping with post-treatment remodeling, unchanged. The left base of tongue appears normal. The valleculae are patent bilaterally. The epiglottis, pre-epiglottic fat, and aryepiglottic folds are unremarkable.

Neck Soft Tissues and Lymph Nodes:
Bilateral cervical lymph node chains are surveyed. The right level IIA region shows a small residual lymph node measuring approximately 0.6 cm in short axis, slightly decreased from 0.8 cm on the prior study. This node demonstrates homogeneous enhancement with no internal necrosis, no irregular margins, and no surrounding soft tissue infiltration. These appearances are in keeping with post-treatment fibrotic/involuted nodal tissue rather than viable tumor. No other pathologically enlarged or morphologically suspicious lymph nodes are identified in the right levels I through V, retropharyngeal, or bilateral posterior triangle regions. Left cervical nodal chains are within normal limits.

Salivary Glands:
Both parotid glands show mild volume reduction and increased fatty replacement compared with expected age-normal parenchyma, consistent with radiation-induced parenchymal atrophy following IMRT with bilateral parotid dose-sparing technique. The submandibular glands appear mildly atrophic, right more than left, in keeping with post-radiation change. No focal salivary gland masses.

Larynx and Hypopharynx:
The larynx is normally positioned. The true and false vocal cords appear symmetric without discrete lesion. The pyriform sinuses are patent and symmetric. The hypopharynx is unremarkable.

Thyroid Gland:
The thyroid gland is mildly heterogeneous in echotexture with a small 5 mm hypoattenuating focus in the right lobe that was not present on the prior comparison study. This likely represents a small colloid cyst or benign follicular nodule; however, given the history of prior neck irradiation, this warrants ultrasound correlation. No dominant thyroid mass. No suspicious calcifications.

Vascular Structures:
The carotid arteries are patent bilaterally. Mild wall irregularity and intimal thickening of the bilateral common carotid arteries is noted, right slightly greater than left, consistent with radiation-related vasculopathy in the radiation field. No high-grade stenosis by imaging criteria. No carotid aneurysm or dissection.

Bony Structures:
The visualized cervical spine shows mild multilevel degenerative changes without acute osseous lesion. The mandible and hyoid are intact. No lytic or sclerotic bony lesions are identified in the visualized skeleton.

Soft Tissue Planes:
The platysma, strap musculature, and paraspinal muscles show post-radiation fibrotic stranding in the bilateral anterior and posterior neck, unchanged from prior. No fascial space collection, abscess, or drainable fluid collection.

Chest Apex (Limited Views):
The lung apices and visualized superior mediastinum are unremarkable. No apical pulmonary nodules or pleural effusion on the included images.

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of residual or recurrent tumor at the right base of tongue primary site. Post-radiation mucosal and submucosal changes are stable and expected for this interval.

2. The right level IIA lymph node has continued to decrease in size (now approximately 0.6 cm, previously 0.8 cm), with benign-appearing morphology consistent with post-treatment involution. No new or suspicious cervical adenopathy identified.

3. Stable post-radiation changes throughout the oropharynx, bilateral parotid and submandibular glands, and anterior/posterior neck soft tissues.

4. Small hypoattenuating nodule in the right thyroid lobe, new since prior study, likely benign; ultrasound correlation recommended given prior radiation exposure to the neck.

5. Mild bilateral carotid wall irregularity and intimal thickening, consistent with early radiation-induced vasculopathy; no hemodynamically significant stenosis on this study.

Overall, no imaging evidence of locoregional disease recurrence at this 18-month post-treatment surveillance timepoint.

Reporting Radiologist: [Attending Radiologist, Head and Neck Imaging]
Dictated and electronically signed.
